Tafseer:
Testimony to the inevitability of the punishment:
'Mursalat' means 'those sent forth'. This word is used here for the winds. Although some have interpreted it as angels, the subsequent attributes—as will become clear—refuse this. This view is based on the misunderstanding that since the particle 'wa' is for an oath, and according to common belief, an oath should be by something sacred, they interpreted 'mursalat' as angels. However, we have explained in detail in various places that oaths in the Qur'an are mostly testimonies—serving as proofs of the claim. This oath is also of this nature. Just as in Surah Adh-Dhariyat, the oath by the winds is sworn to prove the truth of the punishment and recompense, similarly this oath is sworn to prove the inevitability of the promised punishment and the Resurrection.
The word 'urf' refers to the mane of a horse that hangs over its forehead. This is a well-known word in this meaning. Imru' al-Qays's famous verse states:
'When we arose from eating the roasted meat, we wiped our hands on the manes of the horses [a'raf al-jiyad].'
By holding the horse's mane, it can be controlled, and by releasing it, it can be allowed to run. In the verse, the winds are compared to horses, and releasing them is compared to releasing their manes, which is a highly eloquent metaphor. The objective is to show that the winds are neither self-operating nor independent; rather, their forelock is in God's hand of power. When He wills, He controls them, and when He wills, He releases them. He states:
(There is no creature but that He holds its forelock [i.e., controls it]) (Hud, 11:56).

'Asf' means running rapidly and blindly. He states:
(until when you are in ships and they sail with them with a good wind and they rejoice therein, there comes a violent wind [reehun 'asif] and the waves come to them from every place...) (Yunus, 10:22).
This describes the second phase of these winds: after being released, they run rapidly and blindly. This obviously refers to the winds that become violent and turn into storms and punishments, devastating entire nations. The history of their unique manifestations has been detailed in the Qur'an, and their devastating impact is experienced daily even today.

The winds carrying rain of mercy:
'Nashr' means spreading, scattering, raising, or growing. This word is used in all these meanings in the Qur'an. Here, it refers to the winds that bring the rain of mercy because they contain various aspects of 'nashr': they raise the clouds, spread them in the atmosphere, scatter His mercy, and revive the earth with vegetation. He states:
(And He is the One Who sends down the rain after they have despaired and spreads His mercy) (Ash-Shura, 42:28).
While the first oath, as noted, was by the storm winds, this oath is by the winds carrying the rain of mercy, upon which the life and survival of this world depend.
Note the grammatical point: 'asifat' (the violent winds) is connected to 'al-mursalat' (those sent forth) with the conjunction 'fa' to show its gradual progress. In contrast, in this verse, the conjunction 'wa' (and) is used, which shows that this is an independent attribute, not the result of the preceding one.

Winds are not independent but subservient:
This is identical to: (And those [angels] who apportion [each] matter) (Adh-Dhariyat, 51:4)—meaning: these winds perform the task of differentiation and division. Sometimes they drive the clouds, and sometimes they disperse them. They fill one region with water, while leaving another parched. This is a proof showing they are not self-operating or independent, but are subject to a superior power. Since this division occurs through 'nashr' (spreading) and after it, they are connected with the conjunction 'fa'.

The reminder of the winds:
That is, along with rain, they also bring a reminder to people.
The matters of which rain serves as a reminder are explained in various places in the Qur'an. Here, keep some prominent aspects in mind:
o It reminds of monotheism and gratitude to Allah, showing the harmony between heaven and earth.
o Its role in providence reminds of our accountability before God.
o By reviving the dead earth, it reminds of the Resurrection and recompense.
o By becoming a mercy for some and a punishment for others, it reminds of His absolute authority, reward, and punishment.

This states the purpose of the manifestations observed through the winds. It is stated: Allah displays these wonders to establish His proof or to awaken people. 'Aw' (or) is for division—showing that the proof is established against those who wish to remain in heedlessness, while a reminder is obtained by those who wish to take heed. In Surah Al-A'raf, a statement of a group of reformers is recorded, clarifying this 'excuse' and 'warning':
(And when a community among them said, 'Why do you advise a people whom Allah is about to destroy or to punish with a severe punishment?' They said, 'To be absolved before your Lord and perhaps they may fear Him.') (Al-A'raf, 107:164).
That is: if these people do not accept our advice, we will be absolved of our duty of advising, and no responsibility will remain on us before Allah. Then, the responsibility will lie on them, and they will be unable to present any excuse for their deviation on the Day of Resurrection; and if they accept our words and fear Allah, that is the objective. This will be a source of blessing for them and for us.

The purpose of the oaths:
This is the subject of the oaths. It is stated: what you are promised is bound to happen. 'Tu'adun' is general, encompassing both promise and warning, but since this Surah is one of warning, the aspect of warning is dominant. That is: the punishment and the Resurrection of which you are warned are inevitable, and you must face them.
The testimony of the winds' manifestations to the punishment and the Resurrection was explained in detail in the previous Surahs; refer to Surah Adh-Dhariyat for details. Here, remember that the deniers of the Resurrection and the punishment are directed to the manifestations of the winds to warn them: do not boast of your power and status. If Allah wills to bring the punishment, He does not need to make great preparation. If He relaxes the reins of the very wind whose rain sustains your life, your existence will be erased in the twinkling of an eye. There have been many nations in this world whom the wind swept away like straw.

Depiction of the terror of the Resurrection:
These verses portray the upheaval and terror of the Resurrection—showing that on that Day, the things in this universe that appear grand, majestic, and permanent will become completely keyless and insignificant. Just as you have seen great cities, palaces, and fortresses devastated by storm winds, similarly on that Day, such an upheaval will occur that stars will be erased, the heaven will split open, and the mountains of the earth will be reduced to dust.
'Tams' means to erase and remove all signs of something. He states:
(Before We obliterate faces and turn them backward) (An-Nisa, 4:47).
'When the stars are obliterated' (fa-idha-n-nujumu tumisat) means: when the stars are made keyless and lose their light. This is expressed elsewhere as: (When the stars fall, losing their light) (At-Tevir, 81:2) and: (When the stars are scattered) (Al-Infitar, 82:2).

'And when the heaven is split open' (wa idha-s-sama'u furijat) - That is: this heaven, in which no rift or crack can be found, and which appears solid and firm, will split open on the Day of Resurrection. Elsewhere, He states: (And the heaven is opened and will become gateways). In Surah Al-Infitar, He states:
(When the heaven is split open) (Al-Infitar, 82:1).

'And when the mountains are blown away' (wa idha-l-jibalu nusifat) - 'Nasf' means to pulverize, grind, or disperse. This word is used in various places in the Qur'an. For example:
(And look at your god to which you remained devoted. We will surely burn it and then blow it away [lanansifannahu] into the sea as dust.) (Ta-Ha, 20:97).
Regarding the mountains, the deniers' question is answered in these words:
(And they ask you about the mountains, so say, 'My Lord will blow them away [yansifuha] as dust, and He will leave the earth a level plain.') (Ta-Ha, 20:105-106).
Elsewhere, it is stated that on the Day of Resurrection, mountains will become like pouring sand dunes (katheebam-maheela) and wool.
While the preceding two verses described the fate of the heaven, this verse points to what will occur on the earth. Among the things of the earth, the highest status in terms of stability, vastness, and grandeur belongs to the mountains. Thus, when the disbelievers mocked the Resurrection, they would ask: 'When the Resurrection comes, what will happen to these mountains? Will it devastate them as well?' Mentioning the end of the mountains here describes the fate of the entire earth—showing that when the mountains, which people regard as permanent, face this end, there is no need to describe the state of other things.

The presentation of the case of the messengers and their nations:
This describes the primary terror, for which the preceding terrors serve as an introduction—namely, on that Day, a time will be appointed for the messengers. Appointing a time for them obviously means that at a specified time, they will present themselves before the divine court, in the presence of their nations, to testify whether they communicated the warning they were commanded to deliver. If they did, what was their nations' response? The collection of the messengers on the Day of Resurrection and their questioning regarding their nations' response is detailed in the Qur'an. We discussed this under Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 109: (On the Day when Allah will assemble the messengers and say, 'What response did you receive?' They will say, 'We have no knowledge. Indeed, You are the Knower of the unseen'). This points to the reality that on that Day, the case of the messengers and their nations will be heard. Allah Almighty will appoint a time for the presentation of the messengers, and summons will be issued for their nations. Refer also to Surah Al-A'raf, verses 6-7, for details.
The significance of the Day of Decision: 'Uqqitat' is a modified form of 'wuqqitat'. In Arabic, such modifications occur. 'Ar-rusulu uqqitat' means a time will be appointed for the messengers. This is a style similar to saying: 'I appointed a servant' (alghani khadiman), meaning: 'appoint a servant for me.'

When the discourse reaches the mention of that Day, this question is placed before the addressees to express its grandeur and terror: do you understand to what great Day We have postponed the presentation of these messengers! Then, He answers: 'For the Day of Judgement [Decision]' (li-yawmi-l-fasl)—meaning: that Day will be the Day of Our court.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 12 and 13 was combined in the original text.]

As we clarified, this style of expression is used to highlight the grandeur and terror of something. Just as the question: 'For what Day was it postponed?' highlights its grandeur, this question highlights the grandeur of the decision of that Day. Thus, its grandeur is recalled twice.

This states the result of the decision of that Day: on that Day, destruction is for those who denied it!
A grammatical point: This verse, as noted, will appear repeatedly in this Surah, and in every place, despite its deep connection with the preceding verse, its status is completely independent. Some have regarded it as the response (jawab) to the conditional clause mentioned above, but this is grammatically incorrect. If it were the response, it would have been preceded by 'fa' (except when the response is a verbal or adverbial clause)—for example: (Then when the Trumpet is blown with a single blast, that Day will be a difficult Day) (Al-Muddaththir, 74:8-9), and elsewhere: (The Day the heaven will sway with a rapid movement and the mountains will pass away, then woe, that Day, to the deniers) (At-Tur, 52:9-11). We deemed explaining this rule necessary because if this sentence is interpreted as the response to the condition, the interpretation will be incorrect. This will be clarified under its subsequent occurrences. Here, the response to the condition is omitted, just as it is omitted in Surah Al-Inshiqaq, verses 1-6. Because the response is omitted, this verse becomes comprehensive of various aspects, appearing after almost every paragraph of this Surah and holding a specific position in each place.

Historical proofs in support of the claim:
This is a reference to the historical proofs in support of the claim: (Indeed, what you are promised is bound to occur) (verse 7). It is asked: Is it not a fact that We destroyed the former generations, and then pursued them with the later generations? This refers to the nation of Noah, Aad, Thamud, and the subsequent nations whose accounts have been detailed in the Qur'an—such as the nation of Lot, Madyan, and the nation of Pharaoh. The meaning is: when history consistently bears witness that We destroyed those nations who denied the messengers and their warnings, why should Our law change regarding today's criminals who are following their way!

'Then We pursue them with the later generations' (thumma nutbi'uhumu-l-akhireen) - In my view, an auxiliary verb is omitted here. Keeping this in mind, the meaning is: after the former generations, We have consistently pursued those of the later generations who followed their way. The consistency of this divine law is a proof that no change can occur in it. This has happened before, and this will happen in the future, and this also proves that the Day of Decision—of which Allah's messengers warned—is bound to occur.

'Thus do We deal with the criminals' (kadhalika naf'alu bil-mujrimeen) - Although this is stated as a general law—showing that We have always dealt with the criminals in this manner and will continue to do so—it contains a specific reference to the Quraysh: We will deal with them in this manner. If they do not abandon their stubbornness, they will face the same end in the Hereafter as all other criminals. Allah's law is uniform for all.

Following this, the repeating verse appears. Its context is completely clear: 'Woe' (wayl) encompasses all the punishments the criminals will face on that Day, as detailed in the Qur'an. Although apparently a brief word, the terror implied in its brevity and ambiguity cannot be fully described by any lengthy detail.

An internal proof within ourselves:
While the preceding proof was historical, this internal proof is drawn from the creation of man in support of the same claim. The Qur'an has used the creation of man to argue for the Resurrection in various ways. For example:
o Referencing his creation from dust and a drop of water, it proves the possibility of the Resurrection and recreation.
o Referencing the power, wisdom, and craftsmanship displayed in his creation, it proves the necessity of recompense.
o Referencing the care He exercised for his sustenance, it proves the necessity of recompense.
o Referencing the capacity to distinguish between good and bad endowed within him, it proves the necessity of a Day of Justice.
While these themes are spread throughout the Qur'an, there is no need to look far. If you look at the themes of the two adjacent Surahs—Al-Qiyamah and Ad-Dahr—you will find the proofs of all these points.
Answering the doubt of the deniers of the Resurrection: 'Did We not create you from a liquid disdained? And We placed it in a firm lodging for a known extent' (alam nakhluq-kum-mim-ma'im-maheen, fa-ja'alnahu fi qararim-makeen, ila qadarim-ma'loom) - This answers the deniers' doubt regarding being resurrected after they have decayed. Addressing them directly, it is asked: Is it not a fact that We created you from a liquid disdained? The meaning is: this is an obvious truth that no one can deny; so when you witness within yourself that your Creator turned an insignificant drop into a human being, why should resurrecting you after death be impossible or difficult for Him? Is the first creation more difficult or the second!
'Maheen' means insignificant and worthless. Describing the liquid by this attribute shows, first, that man's creation was not from any material that is rare or difficult to find, such that recreating him would be difficult. He was created from a worthless and insignificant thing, whose vast treasury is possessed by His power. Second, it draws attention to the great power of the Creator: the God Who can turn a drop of water into a human being, nothing is difficult for Him; He can do all things.

'And We placed it in a firm lodging' (fa-ja'alnahu fi qararim-makeen) - This points to the amazing care His power spends on turning this insignificant drop into a gem. It is stated: We placed it in a safe and secure lodging. 'Qarar' means stability as well as the place of stability. Here, it refers to the place of stability, pointing to the womb, which His power made a safe place specifically for this purpose. The word 'makeen', when used as an attribute of a place, refers to a place that is completely safe and secure from dangers, threats, and unwanted interference.

'For a known extent' (ila qadarim-ma'loom) - That is: We kept it in a secure lodging for a specified term so that the capabilities intended to be created in it could develop. Then, it is solely by Our power and wisdom that We bring it forth from this safe lodging after this specified term.

'And We determined, and excellent are the Potent' (fa-qadarna fa-ni'ma-l-qadiroon) - In my view, the first word is derived from 'qadar' (decreeing/planning) and the second is from 'qudrah' (power). Drawing attention to these wonders of His power in human creation, Allah Almighty stated: see what wonders We displayed in human creation, and how great and superior is Our power! The meaning is: since this superior power is manifest in human creation, why should We be unable to recreate him!

Following this is the repeating verse. Its context is: the doubt regarding being recreated is refuted by their own creation. One Day, they will see it with their eyes, and that Day will be extremely difficult for the deniers, as stated in Surah As-Saffat:
(It will be only a single shout, and they will at once be staring. And they will say, 'O woe to us! This is the Day of Recompense.' [It will be said], 'This is the Day of Judgement which you used to deny.') (As-Saffat, 37:19-21).

The arrangement of sustenance is a proof of recompense:
This argues for the Day of Decision from the arrangement of providence Allah has made for human sustenance in this world. This proof is explained in various places of the Qur'an. Here, remember that the arrangement of providence Allah made for human sustenance—subserviating the heavens and earth for his benefit—requires that he should not be left unbridled and unaccountable. Rather, a Day must come when he is questioned whether he fulfilled the right of Allah's blessings; then, the grateful will be rewarded, and those who used these blessings for rebellion and corruption will be punished.
'Have We not made the earth a container for the living and the dead?' (alam naj'ali-l-arda kifata, ahya'aw-wa amwata) - 'Kifat' means gathering and assembling. In Arabic, 'fi'al' is also used for the instrument of an action; therefore, 'kifat' means the one that gathers. In this meaning, it has assumed the power of an active participle, allowing the subsequent object.
This argues for the Resurrection and recompense from the perspective of both providence and containment. Stating 'for the living' alongside 'and the dead' points to the aspect of containment—showing that just as this earth holds all the living on its surface, it contains all the dead within it. Those who die do not disappear from its boundaries, but are buried within it. His power places them in the custody of this earth as a trust, in whose lap they were born and raised. This arrangement was made so that when the time comes to gather the people, He commands the earth to bring forth what is in its custody, and it will immediately execute His command:
(And when the earth has been extended and has cast out what was within it and become empty) (Al-Inshiqaq, 84:3-4),
and in Surah Az-Zalzalah: (And the earth discharges its burdens) (verse 2). These verses guide to the reality that those who die do not disappear, but are entrusted to the earth. Allah Almighty will recreate them from this very earth whenever He wills, as stated in Surah Ta-Ha:
(From it We created you, and into it We will return you, and from it We will extract you another time.) (55).
Pondering over all these verses, a clearer reality emerges: man was created from the womb of the earth, and when he dies, he is returned to this very womb from which he was created. So since his first creation is not a source of amazement to anyone, why should his second creation be, when his seed is present within the womb of the earth.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 25 and 26 was combined in the original text.]

'And We placed therein lofty, firmly fixed mountains and have given you to drink sweet water' (wa ja'alna feeha rawasiya shamikhatiw-wa asqaynakum-ma'an furata) - This relates to the providence described in: 'Have We not made the earth a container'. Various verses of the Qur'an indicate that in making the earth a resting place, the existence of mountains holds great importance. In Surah An-Naba, He states:
(Have We not made the earth a resting place and the mountains as pegs?) (meaning: is it not a fact that We made the earth a resting place, and fixed mountains in it to keep it balanced?) Elsewhere, He states: (And He cast into the earth firmly fixed mountains, lest it should sway with you) (Luqman, 31:10). Reference is also made to another benefit of the mountains regarding providence—namely: the height of these mountains is highly effective in controlling the winds and clouds and collecting natural reserves of water. Man is dependent on sweet water, and to provide this blessing, His power created these lofty mountains, which He alone is powerful over. Thus, the discourse reaches the proof of the Day of Decision, without needing to state it explicitly.

Thus, the discourse reaches the proof of the Day of Decision. The explicit mention of it was omitted, and the repeating verse was placed: 'Woe, that Day, to the deniers'—meaning: on that Day, destruction is for those who are untroubled and deny the Day of Decision despite witnessing these obvious signs of His providence and power.

The Resurrection before the eyes:
While the preceding verse demonstrated the Day of Decision through signs in nature, these verses portray its depiction so that the deniers can witness it as if it is present. The style of the discourse is as if it is occurring, and they are commanded to proceed to what they used to deny. It is stated: proceed to what you used to deny.

This reveals the terror hidden within: 'proceed to what you used to deny'. It is stated: proceed to a shadow having three columns. 'Dhill' means shadow, but here it refers to the shadow of smoke. In Surah Al-Waqi'ah, He states:
(And shadow of black smoke, neither cool nor beneficial.) (56:43-44).
This shadow of smoke is described by: 'having three columns' (dhee thalathi shu'ab)—showing that this smoke will surround them from all sides except the side from which they are driven, meaning they will be surrounded by smoke from all directions.
Some commentators have derived a point here: the core tendencies of disbelief are three:
(1) Heedlessness of Allah;
(2) Indifference to His creation;
(3) Denying the Resurrection.
In accordance with these three tendencies, three columns of punishment will surround them. This is a subtle point, but these tendencies are interconnected and are not shared identically by all disbelievers. Therefore, there is no need for this forced interpretation. The obvious meaning is that since there are four directions, if they are driven from one direction, they will be surrounded by smoke from the other three directions, meaning they will be surrounded by the punishment of smoke from all sides.

This refutes a delusion that may arise from the word 'shadow' (dhill). It is stated: in this shadow, there will be neither cool shade nor protection from the flames. This is expressed in slightly different words in Surah Al-Waqi'ah, cited above:
(And shadow of black smoke, neither cool nor beneficial)
showing that it will possess all the painful aspects of smoke, but none of the beneficial aspects that might be expected.

'Indeed, it throws sparks [as huge] as a palace' (innaha tarmee bi-shararin ka-l-qasr) - The reference of the pronoun 'innaha' is the Fire implied by the mention of smoke. It is stated: it will throw sparks and flames as huge as a palace. While some have differed regarding the reading and meaning of 'qasr', we prefer the established reading. In our view, it means a palace or castle, which is an excellent comparison for the flames rising from a great Fire—depicting their spread, height, and color. Palaces are generally built on high grounds, appearing bright from a distance.

Here, the pronoun refers to 'sparks' (sharar), grammatically treating it as collective. 'Sharar' is a collective noun, used identically for both masculine and feminine, singular and plural. Here, it carries a plural meaning because it is compared to 'yellow camels' (jimalatun sufr). 'Jimalah' refers to a herd of camels. This comparison highlights both the color and size of the sparks. The restriction 'yellow' (sufr) is used because, against the backdrop of smoke, the color of the sparks resembles the yellowish-brown color of camels.
Following this is the repeating verse. Its context is completely clear: on the Day they face this terrifying scene, destruction is for those who denied it.

Depiction of the criminals' helplessness: Following the depiction of the Resurrection's terror, this portrays the helplessness and silence of the criminals: today they are eloquent in denying the Resurrection, but on that Day, their tongues will be completely silent, and no word will emerge. In the subsequent Surah, He states:
(They will possess no authority to address Him) (Al-Naba, 78:37).
The Qur'an also states that on that Day, their mouths will be sealed, and Allah Almighty will make their limbs speak to testify to their crimes.
'Nor will it be permitted for them to make excuse' (wa la yu'dhanu lahum fa-ya'tadhirun) - That is: neither will they dare to speak on their own, nor will they be permitted to present any excuse.

'Woe, that Day, to the deniers' - Following this description of their helplessness, the repeating verse appears, and its context is completely clear: when they cannot speak on their own nor are permitted to make excuses, what remains for them except destruction and ruin!

This will be addressed to the criminals: 'This is the Day of Judgement [Decision]; We have gathered you and the former generations.' Recall that when the polytheists mocked the Resurrection, they would ask: 'Will our ancestors also be resurrected?' wanting to prove the Resurrection impossible. Addressing this, He stated: We have done what We promised; both you and your ancestors are present before Us!

'So if you have a plan, then plan against Me' (fa-in kana lakum kaydun fa-keedoon) - That is: in the world, you planned various plots to defeat Our Messenger. If you have any plot left, try it today. Both you and your ancestors are present; if they can help, seek their help.
Note that the efforts of the disbelievers to deny the Messenger are described as 'kayd' (plotting). This is because their entire effort was to protect their leadership. They knew the Prophet's words were true, but out of pride and desires, they refused to accept them, fabricating doubts to convince their people that they were defending their ancestral religion, whereas it was merely a plot.

'Woe, that Day, to the deniers' - Following this challenge, the repeating verse appears, and its context is completely clear: after this challenge, their helplessness will be exposed, and they will face the immense destruction resulting from their denial.

The reward of the righteous:
The decision of that Day regarding the criminals was described above—showing that they will be cast into a Fire whose smoke will surround them. In contrast, this describes the reward of the righteous: they will be in shades, springs, and fruits of their choice. Describing the blessings in this manner is to show that they will be surrounded by these blessings, which is a common usage.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 41 and 42 was combined in the original text.]

This will be said to them: 'Eat and drink with satisfaction. This eating and drinking will be wholesome and free from any distress. This is the reward for the deeds you performed in the world.' This implies that what the ungrateful consumed in the world will not be wholesome for them in the Hereafter, but will be a source of misery because they did not fulfill its right; whereas your eating and drinking will be wholesome because you fulfilled your duties.
'Hani'an' means wholesome and beneficial. It is a circumstantial clause (hal) derived from the subsequent verb. Elsewhere, He states: (So consume it with satisfaction and ease) (An-Nisa, 4:4), where the reference is clear.

This verse refers to verse 18 above, where He stated: (Thus do We deal with the criminals). In contrast, He states here: We reward the doers of good in this manner. The analysis of 'muhsin' (doer of good) appeared in its relevant place.

This is the repeating verse, and its context is completely clear. On one hand, the doers of good will enjoy this eternal ease, and on the other hand, the deniers will be cast into the punishment described above. Imagine the distance between the two! When both ends are displayed, only then will those who chose the world realize their immense loss.

Direct address to the leaders of the Quraysh:
Finally, this addresses the leaders of the Quraysh. Although the preceding verses were also intended for them, they were of a general nature. This addresses them directly, warning them: do not be deluded by the prosperity you enjoy in this world, assuming you will receive the same in the Hereafter. Your ease is short-lived. To enjoy the blessings of the Hereafter, one must be a doer of good, whereas you are criminals; and criminals will face the end that has been detailed.
'Woe, that Day, to the deniers' - This repeating verse appears, and its context is completely clear: on that Day, immense destruction is for the criminals.

After warning them, the third person is used again to rebuke them: they use their prosperity as proof that they will enjoy ease in the Hereafter, but when they are invited to bow before God to fulfill the right of this prosperity, they refuse and behave arrogantly. 'Ruku'' (bowing) is used here to represent prayer, which is the highest expression of gratitude and the greatest destroyer of pride.

Following this is the repeating verse, and its context is completely clear: those who are not ready to perform their duties but expect to receive blessings should remember that their desires will never be fulfilled. On that Day, they will see what treatment they face.
Recall also what was stated in Surah Al-Qalam—namely, on the Day of Resurrection, these arrogant individuals will be called to prostrate, but since they did not prostrate in the world, they will be unable to do so on that Day. This will prove that they are from the arrogant, deserving the punishment of the arrogant.

Rebuking the deniers for their stubbornness:
This rebukes the deniers and expresses despair regarding their faith: since they do not believe in this warning communicated by the Qur'an with such clear proofs, what speech after it will they believe! The meaning is: nothing can exceed the Qur'an in terms of its arguments, eloquence, and impact; so since this has not affected their intellect and hearts, what else can affect them! This comforts the Prophet, showing that their disease is incurable. They will not believe through arguments, but will believe only when they see everything with their eyes, and faith at that moment will be of no benefit.
Some have interpreted 'after it' (ba'dahu) to mean: after that Day arrives, what will they believe in? The meaning is: believing in this warning is beneficial today; when that Day arrives, everyone will believe, but it will be of no benefit. Although this interpretation is possible, the first one is more supported by Qur'anic precedents. Elsewhere, He states:
(Then in what statement after Allah and His verses will they believe?) (Al-Jathiyah, 45:6).
While both interpretations are correct and their objective is close, the first one carries a wider meaning. And Allah knows best, and His knowledge is most comprehensive and wise.
